stem:
While reviewing her lab notebook, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: the calculation error was what Priya noticed.
a calculation error was noticed by Priya.
Priya noticed a calculation error.
there was a calculation error Priya noticed.

explanation: The introductory phrase 'While reviewing her lab notebook' must modify the person doing the reviewing. Only choice C places 'Priya' — the reviewer — as the subject directly after the phrase. The other options make the error or an expletive ('there was') the subject, creating a dangling modifier.

stem:
Hiking along the ridge at sunrise, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: Darius spotted a thick mist in the valley below.
a thick mist covered the valley below.
there was mist spotted by Darius in the valley below.
the valley below was shrouded in mist.

explanation: Only a person can hike, so the subject of the main clause must be the hiker. Choice A places 'Darius' directly after the participial phrase. B and D make the mist or the valley the subject (neither can hike), and C uses an expletive 'there was,' leaving the modifier dangling.

stem:
Written in 1847, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: many readers were shocked by Charlotte Bronte's novel Jane Eyre.
there was shock among readers of Charlotte Bronte's Jane Eyre.
Charlotte Bronte's novel Jane Eyre shocked many readers.
Charlotte Bronte shocked many readers with her novel Jane Eyre.

explanation: The participial phrase 'Written in 1847' must modify the thing that was written — the novel itself. Choice C makes 'Charlotte Bronte's novel Jane Eyre' the subject. A and B put readers or an expletive in that slot, and D makes Charlotte Bronte the subject, but she wasn't written in 1847 — her novel was.

stem:
Tired after a long day of field research, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: it was the tent that the biologists set up first.
the tent was the first thing the biologists set up.
setting up the tent was the biologists' first task.
the biologists set up their tent right away.

explanation: 'Tired after a long day of field research' must describe a person who could be tired. Only choice D places 'the biologists' as the subject directly after the modifier. A, B, and C all make the tent or the task of setting it up the subject, which cannot be 'tired' from fieldwork.

stem:
Reaching for the top shelf, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: the old encyclopedia was knocked loose by Maya.
Maya accidentally knocked loose the old encyclopedia.
there fell an old encyclopedia Maya was grabbing.
an old encyclopedia fell as Maya was reaching.

explanation: Only Maya can be reaching for a shelf, so 'Maya' must be the subject directly after the opening participial phrase. Choice B does this. A and D make the encyclopedia the subject (it can't reach), and C uses an expletive construction, leaving the modifier dangling.

stem:
Founded in 1908, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: Pittsburgh residents often visit the Carnegie Museum of Natural History.
the Carnegie Museum of Natural History is one of Pittsburgh's oldest institutions.
the city of Pittsburgh is home to the Carnegie Museum of Natural History.
many visitors come to the Carnegie Museum of Natural History each year.

explanation: The phrase 'Founded in 1908' must modify the thing that was founded in 1908 — the museum. Only choice B makes 'the Carnegie Museum of Natural History' the subject of the main clause. A, C, and D place residents, Pittsburgh, or visitors as the subject, none of which were founded in 1908.

stem:
Barking loudly at the mail carrier, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: there was loud noise from the neighbor's dog.
scratching was heard from the neighbor's dog.
the neighbor's dog lunged against the fence.
the fence was scratched by the neighbor's dog.

explanation: The participial phrase 'Barking loudly at the mail carrier' must modify whatever is doing the barking — the dog. Only choice C places 'the neighbor's dog' as the subject directly after the modifier. A uses 'there was,' B makes 'scratching' the subject, and D makes 'the fence' the subject — none of which can bark.

stem:
A pioneering oceanographer, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: the deep-sea ecosystems off Bermuda were first mapped by Sylvia Earle in the 1970s.
it was Sylvia Earle who mapped the deep-sea ecosystems off Bermuda in the 1970s.
Sylvia Earle first mapped the deep-sea ecosystems off Bermuda in the 1970s.
mapping the deep-sea ecosystems off Bermuda in the 1970s was Sylvia Earle's achievement.

explanation: 'A pioneering oceanographer' is an appositive that must rename a person. Only choice C places 'Sylvia Earle' as the subject directly after it. A makes the ecosystems the subject, B uses the expletive 'it was,' and D makes 'mapping' a gerund subject — none of which is an oceanographer.

stem:
Trained in classical violin before turning to bluegrass, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: the former teachers of Mark O'Connor were surprised by his fiddling.
Mark O'Connor surprised his former teachers with his fiddling style.
the fiddling style of Mark O'Connor surprised his former teachers.
there was surprise among Mark O'Connor's former teachers.

explanation: The participial phrase describes the person who was trained and later switched genres. Only choice B makes 'Mark O'Connor' the subject directly after the modifier. A and C put teachers or his fiddling style as the subject, and D uses an expletive — none of which was trained in classical violin.

stem:
Built without a single nail, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: there is a 1,300-year-old temple in Japan called Horyu-ji.
over 1,300 years have passed since Japanese carpenters built the Horyu-ji temple.
the Horyu-ji temple has stood in Japan for over 1,300 years.
Japanese carpenters have preserved the Horyu-ji temple for over 1,300 years.

explanation: 'Built without a single nail' must modify whatever was built — the temple. Only choice C places 'the Horyu-ji temple' as the subject right after the modifier. A uses 'there is,' B makes 'years' the subject, and D makes 'Japanese carpenters' the subject, none of which were built.

stem:
After analyzing decades of migration data, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: a surprising pattern emerged for ornithologist Elena Vargas.
ornithologist Elena Vargas identified a surprising pattern.
there was a surprising pattern Elena Vargas noticed.
the surprising pattern was one Elena Vargas had noticed.

explanation: Only a person can analyze data, so the subject after the opening phrase must be the analyst. Choice B places 'ornithologist Elena Vargas' as the subject. A, C, and D make the pattern or an expletive the subject — none of which can analyze data.

stem:
Carved from a single block of marble, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: it is Michelangelo's Pieta that fills St. Peter's Basilica with awe.
Michelangelo worked on the Pieta for nearly two years.
visitors to St. Peter's Basilica admire Michelangelo's Pieta.
Michelangelo's Pieta has drawn visitors to St. Peter's Basilica for centuries.

explanation: 'Carved from a single block of marble' must modify the thing that was carved — the sculpture itself. Only choice D makes 'Michelangelo's Pieta' the subject directly after the modifier. A uses the expletive 'it is,' B makes Michelangelo the subject, and C makes visitors the subject — none of which were carved from marble.

stem:
Eager to test her new telescope, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: Amara pointed her new telescope at the Orion Nebula.
pointing at the Orion Nebula was Amara's first choice.
there was the Orion Nebula for Amara to observe.
the Orion Nebula was the first target chosen by Amara.

explanation: The adjective phrase 'Eager to test her new telescope' must describe a person who can feel eagerness. Only choice A places 'Amara' as the subject directly after the modifier. B makes 'pointing' a gerund subject, C uses 'there was,' and D makes 'the Orion Nebula' the subject — none of which can be eager.

stem:
Published anonymously in 1818, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: readers did not know Mary Shelley had written Frankenstein.
there was no author credited on Frankenstein at first.
Mary Shelley did not receive credit for Frankenstein until a later edition.
Frankenstein was not attributed to Mary Shelley until a later edition.

explanation: 'Published anonymously in 1818' must modify whatever was published — the novel itself. Only choice D places 'Frankenstein' as the subject directly after the modifier. A makes 'readers' the subject, B uses an expletive, and C makes 'Mary Shelley' the subject — but Mary Shelley was not published in 1818; her book was.

stem:
Having rehearsed for months, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: there was ease in the cast's opening scene.
performing the opening scene was easy for the cast.
the opening scene felt effortless to the cast.
the cast performed the opening scene with ease.

explanation: Only people can rehearse, so the subject after the opening perfect-participle phrase must be the people who rehearsed. Choice D places 'the cast' right after the modifier. A uses 'there was,' B makes 'performing' a gerund subject, and C makes 'the opening scene' the subject — none of which can rehearse.

stem:
Known for her vivid use of color, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: Amrita Sher-Gil's paintings transformed modern Indian art.
modern Indian art was transformed by Amrita Sher-Gil's paintings.
the transformation of modern Indian art came from Amrita Sher-Gil.
Amrita Sher-Gil transformed modern Indian art with her paintings.

explanation: The phrase 'Known for her vivid use of color' must describe a person — specifically the artist herself, not her paintings. Only choice D makes 'Amrita Sher-Gil' the grammatical subject. A places her paintings as the subject (a possessive is not a subject head), B puts the art form as the subject, and C uses an abstract noun — none of which are 'known for her' use of color.

stem:
Once thought to be extinct for over 65 million years, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: a fisherman off South Africa startled scientists in 1938 by catching a coelacanth.
there was a coelacanth caught by a South African fisherman in 1938.
scientists were startled in 1938 when a fisherman off South Africa caught a coelacanth.
the coelacanth startled scientists when a fisherman off South Africa caught one in 1938.

explanation: Only the coelacanth was believed extinct for 65 million years, so 'the coelacanth' must be the grammatical subject right after the modifier. Choice D does this. A makes 'a fisherman' the subject, B uses the expletive 'there was,' and C makes 'scientists' the subject — none of which were thought extinct.

stem:
A self-taught mathematician who corresponded with G. H. Hardy from rural India, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: there are thousands of identities in Ramanujan's notebooks.
the notebooks of Srinivasa Ramanujan contain thousands of original identities.
thousands of original identities appear in Srinivasa Ramanujan's notebooks.
Srinivasa Ramanujan filled notebooks with thousands of original identities.

explanation: The long appositive 'A self-taught mathematician who corresponded with G. H. Hardy from rural India' can only rename a person. Only choice D makes 'Srinivasa Ramanujan' the grammatical subject. A uses 'there are,' B makes 'notebooks' the subject, and C makes 'identities' the subject — none of which is a mathematician.

stem:
Designed to withstand the vacuum of space and temperatures below $-150^\circ\text{C}$, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: cryogenic testing of the James Webb Space Telescope took months at NASA.
the James Webb Space Telescope underwent months of testing in a cryogenic chamber.
NASA engineers tested the James Webb Space Telescope in a cryogenic chamber for months.
months of cryogenic testing were required before the James Webb Space Telescope could launch.

explanation: The opening participial phrase 'Designed to withstand...' must modify the thing that was engineered to survive space — the telescope itself. Only choice B places 'the James Webb Space Telescope' as the subject directly after the modifier. A makes 'cryogenic testing' the subject, C makes 'NASA engineers' the subject, and D makes 'months of testing' the subject — none of which were designed to withstand space.

stem:
Having spent nearly a decade photographing the Dust Bowl migration, ______

Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.text: it was 'Migrant Mother' by Dorothea Lange that came to define the era.
the image 'Migrant Mother' made Dorothea Lange famous across the country.
Dorothea Lange's iconic 'Migrant Mother' remains a defining image of the 1930s.
Dorothea Lange produced 'Migrant Mother,' a defining image of the 1930s.

explanation: Only a person can spend a decade photographing something, so the subject after the opening perfect-participle phrase must be the photographer. Choice D makes 'Dorothea Lange' the subject directly after the modifier. A uses 'it was,' B makes 'the image' the subject, and C makes a possessive-headed phrase the subject — none of which can photograph anything.
